Super Bulky β-Diketiminato Group 1 Metal Complexes
Thermodynamics of Ni on Thin-Film Titanates

messages.content:
### Lesson 2: Cornerstones in Contemporary Inorganic Chemistry

This section provides an overview of the special issue published in *Inorganics* (2022), which curates a collection of contemporary research in inorganic chemistry. The collection includes 14 original research articles and 4 review papers, bridging traditional coordination chemistry with frontiers in materials science, catalysis, and bioinorganic chemistry [S.8], [S.10].

#### Key Topical Areas:

*   **Coordination Chemistry & Polymers:** Researchers explored the structural diversity of copper(II) complexes, including those using pyridine/pyrazine amides and isomeric terpyridines. These studies illustrate how ligand geometry and donor atom preferences dictate the formation of isolated complexes versus 1D coordination polymers [S.8].
*   **Solid-State & Materials Chemistry:** Investigations included the synthesis of fluoridoaluminates via ammonothermal routes and the creation of the first uranyl borosilicate using hydrothermal synthesis [S.9]. Additionally, the impact of Ni-based catalysts on titanate substrates was analyzed to evaluate thermodynamic stability for fuel cell applications [S.9], [S.10].
*   **Bioinorganic & Organometallic Chemistry:** Key studies focused on medical applications, such as copper-based chemotherapeutics for breast cancer stem cells, and the catalytic mechanisms of [FeFe]-hydrogenase models [S.10]. Organometallic research highlighted advances in f-block element chemistry, specifically the synthesis of polarized-covalent thorium-metal bonds [S.10].
*   **Catalysis & Computational Modeling:** Advanced computational methods, including Density Functional Theory (DFT) and multivariate linear regression (MLR), were applied to rationalize catalyst poisoning in copolymerization and to study bonding in heterometallic clusters [S.9], [S.10].
*   **Review Articles:** Several comprehensive reviews summarized findings in specific domains, including solvothermal synthesis of ceria materials, the redox properties of pyridine-silver complexes, the design of lanthanide-based single-molecule magnets (SMMs), and the role of Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) as heterogeneous catalysts [S.10], [S.11].

**Conclusion:** The collection reflects the interdisciplinary nature of modern inorganic chemistry, demonstrating how fundamental structural studies directly inform applications in energy conversion, cancer treatment, and industrial catalysis [S.8].

Lesson 2 — Cornerstones in Contemporary Inorganic Chemistry - Presentation10 slides
Cornerstones in Contemporary Inorganic Chemistry
  • A collection of seminal research in inorganic chemistry.
  • Highlights key topics defining the early 21st century.
  • Features a mix of original research and review articles.

This document is a printed collection of invited articles from the Special Issue of the journal *Inorganics* titled "Cornerstones in Contemporary Inorganic Chemistry," edited by Duncan Gregory [S.1, S.4, S.5].

**Overview of Content**
The volume compiles diverse research papers covering various subfields of inorganic chemistry, including:

*   **Coordination Chemistry:** Investigations into Cu(II) complexes and coordination polymers featuring pyridine/pyrazine amides, as well as the structural assembly of terpyridines with [Cu(hfacac)$_2$] units [S.6].
*   **Cluster Compounds:** A study on the synthesis and bonding of pentagonal bipyramidal clusters containing a cyclo-Sn$_5$ ring [S.6].
*   **Synthesis and Characterization:** Research on bulky $\beta$-diketiminato Group 1 metal complexes, a silver cyanoguanidine nitrate hydrate compound, and novel fluoridoaluminate modifications synthesized via the ammonothermal method [S.6].
*   **Materials and Catalysis:** Structural investigation of a crystalline uranyl borosilicate under hydrothermal conditions, a thermodynamic profile of nickel on thin-film titanates (ATiO$_3$), and the use of multivariate linear regression to predict monomer poisoning in transition metal catalysis [S.6].

**Publication Information**
*   **Editor:** Duncan Gregory (University of Glasgow) [S.5].
*   **Publisher:** MDPI [S.4, S.5].
*   **Licensing:** All articles are Open Access, distributed under CC BY or CC BY-NC-ND licenses to facilitate academic dissemination [S.5].

This special issue of *Inorganics*, titled "Cornerstones in Contemporary Inorganic Chemistry," presents a collection of 14 original research articles and 4 review papers covering diverse fields of inorganic chemistry.

The key contributions are summarized below:

Coordination and Organometallic Chemistry
Research in this area spans from transition metal complexes to f-block chemistry. Key findings include the preferential coordination of O-atoms in amide ligands for Cu(II) complexes, the use of terpyridine isomers to control the formation of coordination polymers, and the synthesis of heterometallic clusters with pentagonal bipyramidal cores. Advances in organometallic chemistry feature the structural characterization of Th–Re and Th–Ru bonds, which exhibit more ionic character than their uranium counterparts, as well as the use of flexible tris-anilido ligands for small molecule activation.

Solid-State and Materials Chemistry
Studies in this field focus on synthetic methodologies and their applications. Highlights include the use of ammonothermal synthesis to produce fluoridoaluminate polymorphs and hydrothermal methods for creating uranyl borosilicates for nuclear waste management. Additionally, the issue examines the efficacy of Ni-supported perovskite catalysts for fuel cells and the development of new Ag(I) coordination species.

Bioinorganic Chemistry and Catalysis
Several studies address biological and catalytic applications. Copper(II) complexes with naphthol Schiff base ligands were identified as potential breast cancer stem cell therapeutics, outperforming existing agents. Mechanistic studies using DFT and TDDFT explored the photochemistry of $[FeFe]$-hydrogenase models, offering insights into preventing irreversible enzyme photodamage. Furthermore, research into heterogeneous catalysis analyzed poisoning effects in ethylene copolymerization and the role of Metal Organic Frameworks (MOFs) in olefin epoxidation and $CO_2$ cycloaddition.

Review Articles
The issue provides comprehensive reviews on the following topics:
1. Solvothermal pathways for synthesizing and morphologically tuning cerium dioxide ($CeO_2$) and substituted ceria.
2. The synthesis, structure, and redox properties of pyridine-silver tetraoxometallates ($AgXO_4$, where $X = Cl, Mn, Re$).
3. The design and magnetic properties of Lanthanide-based Single-Molecule Magnets (SMMs) derived from Schiff base ligands.
4. The application of MOFs as heterogeneous catalysts, specifically focusing on the relationship between catalytic performance and structural building blocks.

(pp. 129-305)

This summary covers the research article by Manfroni et al. found on pages 27–46.

**Key Points of the Study:**

The researchers investigated the coordination chemistry of isomeric terpyridines and their impact on the assembly of coordination polymers when reacting with $[Cu(hfacac)_2]$ (where Hhfacac is hexafluoropentane-2,4-dione).

**Structural Isomerism:**
The study focuses on comparing the following two isomeric ligands:
1. $4,2':6',4''$-terpyridine
2. $3,2':6',3''$-terpyridine

These ligands were synthesized with isomeric $4'$-trifluoromethylphenyl substituents. The work explores how the positioning of the nitrogen atoms in the terpyridine backbone influences the resulting supramolecular architectures.

**Coordination Behavior:**
The research demonstrates that the structural orientation of the ligands significantly dictates the geometry of the copper complexes. By using $[Cu(hfacac)_2]$ as a secondary building unit, the authors provide detailed crystallographic data showing how these specific substituted terpyridines lead to varying polymeric networks.

**Observations:**
The assembly processes are highly sensitive to the peripheral steric and electronic effects introduced by the trifluoromethylphenyl groups. The paper provides a comprehensive analysis of the molecular structures obtained, helping to map the relationship between ligand design and the topology of the final coordination polymers.

This section focuses on the research article titled "Synthesis, Structure and Bonding in Pentagonal Bipyramidal Cluster Compounds Containing a cyclo-Sn5 Ring, [(CO)3MSn5M(CO)3]^{4-} (M = Cr, Mo)".

Key points include:

1. Synthesis and Molecular Geometry: The study details the preparation and structural analysis of pentagonal bipyramidal clusters. These clusters incorporate a cyclo-Sn5 ring, which is effectively capped by metal-based units.

2. Chemical Composition: The core structure is represented by the formula $[(CO)_3MSn_5M(CO)_3]^{4-}$, where the transition metals involved are Chromium (Cr) or Molybdenum (Mo).

3. Bonding Analysis: The researchers investigated the bonding characteristics within these cluster compounds. The inclusion of the $[Sn_5]^{6-}$ aromatic-like ring in a pentagonal bipyramidal arrangement provides insights into the stability and electronic nature of these anionic species.

Overall, the work contributes to the understanding of stannapolyhedral clusters and the interaction between transition metal carbonyl fragments and main-group element rings (p. 47).

The document section covering pages 59–68 focuses on the following key points:

Synthesis and Characterization of Super Bulky $\beta$-Diketiminato Group 1 Metal Complexes
This research, authored by Dafydd D. L. Jones, Samuel Watts, and Cameron Jones, details the preparation and structural analysis of group 1 metal complexes utilizing sterically demanding $\beta$-diketiminato ligands (p. 59).

Silver Cyanoguanidine Nitrate Hydrate
Authored by Xianji Qiao, Alex J. Corkett, Dongbao Luo, and Richard Dronskowski, this paper investigates the compound $Ag(C_2N_4H_4)NO_3 \cdot \frac{1}{2} H_2O$ (p. 69). The study highlights the structural properties of this cyanoguanidine compound, specifically noting its coordination through an inner nitrogen atom (p. 69).

This section covers the synthesis and characterization of various inorganic compounds, focusing on structural investigations and unique coordination modes:

The study regarding silver cyanoguanidine nitrate hydrate explores the compound $Ag(C_2N_4H_4)NO_3 \cdot 1/2 H_2O$. Researchers identified that the cyanoguanidine molecule coordinates to the silver center specifically through an inner nitrogen atom.

In the realm of solid-state chemistry, the text discusses ammonothermal synthesis as a method for producing novel fluoridoaluminates. This process resulted in two distinct structural modifications of $K_2AlF_5$ as well as the elpasolite-structured compound $Rb_2KAlF_6$.

Finally, the document details the hydrothermal synthesis and structural analysis of a crystalline uranyl borosilicate, contributing to the broader understanding of complex actinide-based minerals and synthetic crystalline frameworks.

This section covers research on several distinct areas of inorganic chemistry, primarily focusing on synthesis and structural characterization:

**Novel Fluoridoaluminates**
The study by Bäucker et al. investigates fluoridoaluminate systems synthesized via ammonothermal methods. Key findings include the identification of two distinct modifications of $K_{2}AlF_{5}$ and the successful synthesis of the elpasolite structure $Rb_{2}KAlF_{6}$ (p. 77).

**Uranyl Borosilicate**
Pace et al. detail the hydrothermal synthesis of a crystalline uranyl borosilicate. This research provides a thorough structural investigation, expanding the understanding of uranium-based materials synthesized in aqueous environments (p. 91).

**Thermodynamic Investigation of Nickel Titanates**
Lin et al. examine the thermodynamics of nickel supported on thin-film titanates with the formula $ATiO_{3}$. The work focuses on behavioral characteristics of these systems, providing insights into their surface interactions and stability (p. 105).

**Monomer Poisoning Models**
Zhao et al. present a study on ethylene/polar monomer copolymerization catalyzed by late transition metals. The researchers developed multivariate linear regression models to effectively predict the "monomer poisoning effect," which is a critical factor in catalytic efficiency (p. 119).

The section spanning pages 91–104 (corresponding to the work by Pace et al.) details the hydrothermal synthesis and structural investigation of a crystalline uranyl borosilicate. 

Key technical aspects include:

1. Synthesis: The authors utilized a hydrothermal method to produce the crystalline material, which involves placing precursors under high-pressure aqueous conditions at elevated temperatures.

2. Structural Characterization: The study focuses on determining the atomic arrangement of the resulting uranyl borosilicate. The structure is characterized by the presence of the uranyl moiety, defined by the formula $$[UO_2]^{2+}$$, integrated into a borosilicate framework.

3. Objective: The research aimed to provide a comprehensive structural investigation to better understand the bonding and coordination environments within this specific radioactive-element compound. This work contributes to the broader field of actinide chemistry by analyzing the complex interplay between borosilicate networks and uranium centers.

The research article found on pages 105–118, titled "A Thermodynamic Investigation of Ni on Thin-Film Titanates ($ATiO_3$)", focuses on the interaction between nickel and titanate surfaces. 

Key points of the study include:

Analysis of Nickel-Titanate Interfaces: The authors investigate the thermodynamic properties of Ni supported on thin-film perovskite-structured titanates ($ATiO_3$). This investigation is significant for understanding the behavior of catalysts in high-temperature chemical processes.

Material Systems: The research explores how the metal-support interaction varies based on the choice of the A-site cation in the $ATiO_3$ perovskite framework. By examining these thin-film systems, the researchers aim to better define the energetic landscape of metal dispersion and stability on oxide supports.

Thermodynamic Insights: The study utilizes experimental and theoretical approaches to quantify the interaction energies between the Ni phase and the titanate substrates. These findings contribute to the broader goal of predicting and improving the performance and longevity of metal-based catalysts in energy-relevant applications.

The document section from pages 119 to 344 encompasses a variety of research topics in inorganic chemistry, specifically focusing on catalysis, medicinal applications, and advanced structural characterization. Key areas covered include:

Catalysis and Material Science
Researchers utilized multivariate linear regression models to predict monomer poisoning effects in the copolymerization of ethylene and polar monomers catalyzed by late transition metals (p. 119). Further studies explored the synthesis of cerium dioxide materials via solvothermal routes (p. 217) and the application of Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) as heterogeneous catalysts for olefin epoxidation and carbon dioxide cycloaddition (p. 305).

Biological and Medicinal Inorganic Chemistry
Studies focused on the synthesis of Copper(II) complexes, specifically those featuring Naphthol Schiff bases and polypyridyl ligands, which exhibit efficiency as breast cancer stem cell-active agents (p. 129). Additionally, the aqueous solution equilibria and spectral properties of copper complexes involving tripeptides (composed of glycine, sarcosine, leucine, or phenylalanine) were investigated (p. 143).

Structural Studies and Molecular Modeling
Significant research was dedicated to investigating the photochemistry of $[FeFe]$-hydrogenase models using DFT and TDDFT calculations, specifically examining $Fe_2(S_2C_3H_6)(CO)_6(\mu-CO)$ and its oxidized forms (p. 173). Other structural works include the synthesis and characterization of polarized-covalent bonds between thorium-rhenium and thorium-ruthenium (p. 191), the encapsulation of U(III), U(IV), and La(III) cations using functionalized tris(anilido)triazacyclononanes (p. 201), and an overview of pyridine-complexed $AgXO_4$ compounds where $X$ represents $Cl$, $Mn$, or $Re$ (p. 251). The section concludes with research on lanthanide-based single-molecule magnets derived from salicylaldehyde-based Schiff base ligands (p. 265).
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: In the work by Housecroft et al., what determines whether the coordination polymers formed by terpyridine isomers pack via C–F···F–C contacts or π–π stacking?
options: The choice of the central metal ion
The specific isomer geometry and ligand conformation
The solvent used during the hydrothermal synthesis
The oxidation state of the copper center
questions.explanation: The study highlights that the positioning of N donor atoms in the terpyridine isomers dictates their geometry (V-shape vs others), which in turn dictates the packing interactions available to the resulting structures.

questions.question: Regarding the study of [(CO)3MSn5M(CO)3]4− (M = Cr, Mo) clusters, what does the computational analysis reveal about the LUMO distribution?
options: It is always localized on the apical metal atoms
It is governed entirely by the oxidation state of the tin ring
It depends on the subtle balance between Sn−Sn π-bonding and trans-annular M-M bonding
It is independent of the metal center used
questions.explanation: McGrady et al. found that the LUMO localization is sensitive to the electronic structure; while their specific clusters were dominated by Sn-Sn π-bonding, other isoelectronic clusters shift the LUMO to the metal apices.

questions.question: Why did the researchers investigate the bonding of the silver cyanoguanidine nitrate hydrate complex using first principles calculations?
options: Because the experimental result of inner nitrogen coordination contradicted the expectation that terminal coordination is more favorable
Because the crystals were too unstable to be analyzed by standard X-ray crystallography
Because the complex exhibited unexpected metallic conductivity
Because the cyanoguanidine ligand could not be synthesized in laboratory conditions
questions.explanation: The researchers found that bonding through the inner nitrogen was unexpected and energetically less favorable than the standard terminal coordination, necessitating theoretical confirmation.

questions.question: What is the primary scientific implication of the study on Ni supported on ATiO3 titanates for CO2 reforming of CH4?
options: Perovskites are superior to MgAl2O4 due to their higher catalytic activity
The choice of alkaline earth metal (A) in the perovskite significantly alters catalytic performance and stability
Ni catalysts on titanates are immune to oxidation under all reaction conditions
Atomic layer deposition prevents any interaction between the metal and the support
questions.explanation: The study revealed that the interaction between the nickel catalyst and the titanate support varies with the alkaline earth metal (Ca, Sr, Ba), directly affecting catalyst deactivation and performance.

questions.question: What role does the study of [FeFe]-hydrogenase models (Fe2(S2C3H6)(CO)6) serve in understanding enzyme photo-damage?
options: It proves that CO inhibits the enzyme by permanently bonding to the sulfur bridge
It suggests that excited state decay involves photo-dissociation of a CO ligand to prevent irreversible damage
It demonstrates that UV light has no effect on the catalytic site of hydrogenases
It confirms that the hydrogenase site is structurally identical to simple Fe-CO complexes
questions.explanation: The TDDFT investigation suggests that the enzyme prevents photo-damage by facilitating a decay mechanism through CO dissociation when exposed to excessive light, rather than undergoing irreversible degradation.

cards.front: What is the primary aim of the special issue 'Cornerstones in Contemporary Inorganic Chemistry'?
cards.back: To assemble a collection of seminal articles representing key aspects and exciting research areas in early 21st-century inorganic chemistry.

cards.front: In the study of Cu(II) complexes with pyridine/pyrazine amides, through which atom does the metal preferentially coordinate?
cards.back: The donor O atom of the amide group, rather than the nitrogen of the amide NH2 group.

cards.front: How do isomeric terpyridines (4,2':6',4'' vs 3,2':6',3'') differ in their coordination polymer packing?
cards.back: The 4,2':6',4''-terpyridine chains (V-shaped) are held by C–F···F–C contacts, while 3,2':6',3''-terpyridine chains use π–π stacking.

cards.front: What structural core describes the clusters [(CO)3MSn5M(CO)3]4- (M = Cr, Mo)?
cards.back: A pentagonal bipyramidal core consisting of a Sn5(4-) ring bridging two zero-valent M(CO)3 fragments.

cards.front: What is unique about the structure of silver(I) cyanoguanidine nitrate hydrate, Ag(C2N4H4)NO3 · 1/2 H2O?
cards.back: It is the first example of a monovalent silver complex coordinating through the inner, rather than terminal, nitrogen atoms of the cyanoguanidine ligand.

cards.front: What technique was used by Niewa et al. to prepare the fluoridoaluminates K2AlF5 and Rb2KAlF6?
What structural feature characterizes the uranyl borosilicate K1.8Na1.2[(UO2)BSi4O12] reported by Zur Loye et al.?
cards.back: It is a 3D framework constructed from isolated UO6 octahedra and infinite chains of [BSi4O12](5-) anions (vertex-sharing BO4 and SiO4 tetrahedra).

cards.front: What was the main finding of Gorte et al. regarding Ni catalysts supported on ATiO3 (A = Ca, Sr, Ba) titanates?
cards.back: The perovskite samples were less active than Ni on MgAl2O4 alone, and the Ni/CaTiO3 catalyst deactivated during CO2 reforming of CH4.

cards.front: What factors were identified by Luo et al. as mediating the poisoning effects of polar monomers on late transition metal catalysts?
cards.back: Electron-rich functional groups (carbonyl, carboxyl, acyl) and the metal-X distance in the σ-coordination structure.

cards.front: How do the copper(II) complexes described by Ortu et al. compare to salinomycin in treating breast cancer stem cells?
cards.back: Two of the synthesized complexes were found to be more than four times more effective at killing breast cancer stem cells than the established agent salinomycin.

slides.title: Cornerstones in Contemporary Inorganic Chemistry
bullets: A collection of seminal research in inorganic chemistry.
Highlights key topics defining the early 21st century.
Features a mix of original research and review articles.
Showcases the expansive, cross-cutting nature of the field.
slides.notes: This collection, edited by Duncan Gregory, gathers essential research representing the cutting edge of modern inorganic chemistry. It covers topics ranging from coordination complexes to industrial catalysts.

slides.title: Copper(II) Complexes and Coordination Polymers
bullets: Utilizes pyridine, pyrazine amide, and amino benzamide ligands.
Features hard and soft donor atoms for versatile bonding.
Explores connections to metalloenzyme structures.
Bridges molecular complexes and inorganic polymeric frameworks.
slides.notes: Research by Wackerbarth et al. investigates copper(II) systems, demonstrating how structural variety in ligands allows for both isolated coordination and complex polymeric assemblies.

slides.title: Isomeric Terpyridines in Assembly
bullets: Examines 4,2':6',4''- and 3,2':6',3''-terpyridine isomers.
Incorporates 4'-trifluoromethylphenyl substituents for structural control.
Investigates assembly motifs with [Cu(hfacac)2] units.
Demonstrates how isomerism dictates coordination polymer architecture.
slides.notes: Manfroni et al. demonstrate how small changes in ligand isomerism can profoundly shift the final assembly of coordination polymers with copper-based building blocks.

slides.title: Pentagonal Bipyramidal Cluster Compounds
bullets: Features cluster compounds with a cyclo-Sn5 ring.
Synthesis of [(CO)3MSn5M(CO)3]4- where M is Cr or Mo.
Explores complex electronic structure and internal bonding.
Advances understanding of tin-based cluster stability.
slides.notes: Mondal et al. analyze the synthesis and bonding of unique metal-tin clusters, shedding light on the structural stability of pentagonal bipyramidal arrangements.

slides.title: Super Bulky β-Diketiminato Group 1 Complexes
bullets: Focuses on group 1 metal complexes with bulky ligands.
Synthesis using advanced β-diketiminato structural design.
Characterization of unique steric and electronic properties.
Extends the scope of ligand-stabilized alkali metal chemistry.
slides.notes: Jones et al. provide insights into how super-bulky ligands protect and stabilize group 1 metal complexes, a challenging area of synthetic inorganic chemistry.

slides.title: Silver Cyanoguanidine Nitrate Hydrate
bullets: Details the structure of Ag(C2N4H4)NO3·1/2H2O.
Cyanoguanidine coordinates via an inner nitrogen atom.
Novel insights into silver-based nitrogen coordination.
Highlights structural diversity in silver cyanoguanidine complexes.
slides.notes: Qiao et al. report on the specific coordination geometry of this silver compound, emphasizing the versatile role of cyanoguanidine as a ligand.

slides.title: Fluoridoaluminates via Ammonothermal Synthesis
bullets: Novel synthesis of K2AlF5 and Rb2KAlF6 elpasolite.
Utilizes ammonothermal conditions for material growth.
Identifies new modifications of existing fluoridoaluminates.
Advancing chemical synthesis in non-aqueous solvent systems.
slides.notes: Bäucker et al. showcase how the ammonothermal method allows for the synthesis of new fluoride-based materials, expanding the library of accessible ceramics.

slides.title: Crystalline Uranyl Borosilicate
bullets: Hydrothermal synthesis of crystalline uranyl borosilicates.
Structural investigation of uranium-based framework materials.
Provides data on complex actinide coordination environments.
Contributes to actinide materials chemistry and design.
slides.notes: Pace et al. detail the hydrothermal growth and structural analysis of uranyl borosilicates, relevant for actinide research and geological materials.

slides.title: Thermodynamics of Ni on Titanates
bullets: Investigation of Ni metals on ATiO3 thin-films.
Study of thermodynamic surface and interface interactions.
Crucial for understanding catalyst surface reactivity.
Bridges solid-state chemistry and surface catalysis.
slides.notes: Lin et al. examine the thermodynamic behavior of nickel on titanate substrates, a study that offers valuable information for the design of heterogeneous catalysts.

slides.title: Predicting Monomer Poisoning in Catalysis
bullets: Uses multivariate linear regression modeling techniques.
Focuses on late transition metal ethylene copolymerization.
Quantifies the impact of polar monomer poisoning.
Predictive modeling streamlines future catalyst development.
slides.notes: Zhao et al. apply statistical modeling to understand how polar monomers inhibit transition metal catalysis, providing a tool for optimizing industrial polymerization processes.
